mūla.devanāgarī: सर्वतः पाणि-पादं तत् सर्वतो ऽक्षि-शिरो-मुखम् | सर्वतः श्रुतिमल् लोके सर्वम् आवृत्य तिष्ठति
mūla.iast: sarvataḥ pāṇi-pādaṃ tat sarvato 'kṣi-śiro-mukham | sarvataḥ śrutimal loke sarvam āvṛtya tiṣṭhati
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 13 (Kṣetra-Kṣetrajña-Vibhāga-Yoga (The Yoga of Distinction Between Field and Field-Knower)), verse 13
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: इति। लोके यद् वस्तुजातं तत् सर्वं व्याप्य तिष्ठति परिशुद्धस्वरूपं देशादिपरिच्छेदरहिततया सर्वगतम् इत्यर्थः।।

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.divergence_note: Shankara explicitly rejects the alternative reading 'anadimad mat-param' (taking 'mat' as referring to Vasudeva-shakti) as apavyakhyana — a distorted reading — because attributing a specific shakti to Brahman violates the total negation of vishesha that this verse establishes.
advaita.english_rendering: Brahman — the supreme knowable (jneya) — is anadi (without beginning) because it is not constituted by any prior cause; it transcends the reach of the words 'sat' (being) and 'asat' (non-being) because all words function only through the fourfold nexus of jati (class), kriya (action), guna (quality), or sambandha (relation), none of which apply to Brahman. This is not a contradiction: Brahman is neither the object of the cognition 'it is' nor 'it is not,' being beyond the senses and thus beyond both forms of buddhi (cognition). The Upanishads confirm: 'neti neti' — Brahman is designated only by negation, as that which is 'other than the known and above the unknown' (Kena 1.3).

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: The knowable is parasuddha-atman — the perfectly pure self — which, though itself without hands and feet in the ordinary sense, is the inner controller (antaryamin) capable of the function of all hands, feet, eyes, and ears everywhere. This Brahman pervades (avrtya tishthati) all that exists in the cosmos, yet remains spatially unlimited (desha-pariccheda-rahita). The liberated soul, on attaining 'sadharmmyam' with Bhagavan (Gita 14.2), itself attains this all-pervasive functional omnipotence — a fact grounded in shruti: 'sa ya evam vit... niranjanah paramam samyam upaiti' (Mundaka 3.1.3).
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Ramanuja does not read 'na sat tan nasad' as an absolute negation of all predication; rather, Brahman transcends ordinary sat/asat because its nature as vishishta (qualified by cit and acit) exceeds the binary. Brahman is supremely real, not beyond reality.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva does not engage the sat/asat dialectic at length; for him, Brahman's transcendence of sat/asat means only that ordinary categories of finite existence do not capture Hari's infinite svabhava — not that Brahman is beyond existence as such.
dvaita.english_rendering: The 'param Brahma' here is Hari alone — the supreme independent Lord (svantantra) established by the preceding verse's promise ('sa ca yah,' 13.4). 'Anadimad' signals that Brahman has no external cause or prior: unlike the body which is 'adimad' (having a beginning), Brahman is eternal and self-caused. Hari's 'anyatya-prabhava' — His distinctive potency unlike any other — is precisely what is indicated. The jiva, by contrast, is always 'anyadhina' (dependent), utterly distinct and perpetually subordinate to Hari.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: The knowable Brahman (jneya) is Krishna himself, disclosed through six groups of his own svarupa-gunas (qualities intrinsic to his nature). 'Anadimad' means: Krishna as para-Brahman is the foundation of all Vedanta; there is no prior to him, for he is sva-prakasha (self-luminous). The phrase 'na sat tan nasad ucyate' reveals that Krishna's svarupa is the ashraya (ground) of two apparently contradictory dharmas — being and non-being — yet neither exhausts him: as Bhagavata proclaims, 'na hi virodha ubhayam bhagavaty apariganita-guna-gana Ishvare' — there is no contradiction in the Lord of uncountable gunas. Krishna's nature exceeds all categorical containment.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha diverges sharply from Shankara: Brahman is not nirvishesha (featureless) but svishesha (fully qualified) — the 'na sat na asat' formula is a floor, not a ceiling; it rules out finite categories while leaving Krishna's positive gunas intact.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.divergence_note: Sridhara's 'mat-param' reading points toward a personal ground for the impersonal Brahman — Vishnu is the named antecedent of 'mat' — which Shankara rejects as apavyakhyana. The devotional school thus retains a personal anchor even within the apophatic frame.
bhakti.english_rendering: The knowable (jneya) is para-nirvishesha Brahman — Vishnu's transcendent, featureless aspect — which the seeker who desires moksha should know through the sadhanas enumerated in prior verses. It is 'anadimad': the compound either reads as a bahuvrihi (that which has no adi, i.e., beginning) making the matup suffix metrically redundant, or it splits as two words — 'anadih' and 'mat-param' — meaning 'my (Vishnu's) supreme featureless rupa.' The verse then says 'na sat na asat': what is posited by vidhi-pramana (injunctive knowledge) is called sat; what is posited by nishedha-pramana (prohibitive knowledge) is asat; this jneya, being the ground of both without being captured by either, is described only by exclusion.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusudana's synthesis holds: Brahman is nirvishesha (Shankara), yet Krishna can 'pravakshyami' (speak it) through lakshana-vrtti — indirect denotation. Bhakti-rasa is not surrendered; the devotee's Krishna is the very svarupa that transcends both sat and asat.
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: The jneya is nirvishesha-brahma-vastu — the objectless pure-consciousness object — about which Krishna promises clarity (pravakshyami). 'Anadimad' is read by some as 'anadih mat-param' (my supreme formless aspect) — but Madhusudana explicitly rejects the alternative reading 'aham Vasudeva-akhya para shaktir yasya' as apavyakhyana, since this verse's entire purpose is to negate all vishesha in Brahman. 'Na sat na asat' means: 'sat' is what is the object of vidhi-pramana (affirmative cognition), 'asat' is what is the object of nishedha-pramana (negative cognition); Brahman, as svaprakasha-chaitanya (self-luminous consciousness) beyond both, falls under neither. All four modes of word-application — by jati, kriya, guna, sambandha — are unavailable, hence 'yato vacho nivartante' (Taittiriya 2.4.9).

vishishtadvaita.divergence_note: Ramanuja does not read 'na sat tan nasad' as an absolute negation of all predication; rather, Brahman transcends ordinary sat/asat because its nature as vishishta (qualified by cit and acit) exceeds the binary. Brahman is supremely real, not beyond reality.

shuddhadvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha diverges sharply from Shankara: Brahman is not nirvishesha (featureless) but svishesha (fully qualified) — the 'na sat na asat' formula is a floor, not a ceiling; it rules out finite categories while leaving Krishna's positive gunas intact.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If Brahman cannot be called 'sat' (existing) or 'asat' (non-existing), what epistemic status does the Gita's own claim to teach about it actually have?
Shankara says words function only via jati, kriya, guna, or sambandha — and none applies to Brahman. Is the Gita itself then pointing at its own limit?
When Ramanuja says Brahman transcends sat/asat because it is 'vishishta' rather than nirvishesha, is that a different answer or a different question from Shankara's?
If Madhva's Hari is beyond sat/asat while remaining the fully independent Lord with definite svabhava, does 'na sat na asat' mean something categorically different for Dvaita than for Advaita?
Vallabha's Krishna is the ashraya (ground) of both contradictory dharmas — does this make 'na sat na asat' a sign of abundance rather than absence?
Madhusudana allows lakshana-vrtti (indirect denotation) to rescue scriptural transmission of the unspeakable — does that rescue apply to all schools equally, or only to the nirvishesha position?
If the knowable (jneya) is that by knowing which one attains amritatvam, and the knowable is beyond all predication, what does 'knowing' it actually consist in?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you catch yourself insisting 'I know exactly what I am' or 'I know exactly what I am not' — career, role, relationship — notice that both the affirmation and the negation are equally incomplete. The jñani's practice here is to hold the open question without collapsing it: neither claiming full self-knowledge nor denying that there is something to know.
everyday_applications.vishishtadvaita: Your deepest self is not the bounded person you experience on an ordinary day. Ramanuja's point is that the pure atman, when liberated, participates in Bhagavan's all-pervasive functioning. In practice: when you act in service (kainkarya), stop tracking the limits of your capacity — act as though the Lord's hands and eyes are operating through you, because the shruti says they can.
everyday_applications.dvaita: You are not Hari. This is not a humiliation — it is the architecture of relationship. Madhva's application: in work, in family, in practice — identify clearly what is your responsibility and what is the Lord's domain. Dependency on Hari is not weakness; it is the accurate map of how reality is structured. Confusing yourself with the source of power is the root error.
everyday_applications.shuddhadvaita: The verse's 'na sat na asat' points to Krishna's inexhaustibility. Vallabha's application: do not reduce your encounter with Krishna — in scripture, image, kirtan, or memory — to a single fixed meaning. The Lord's gunas are ananta (infinite); every encounter can reveal a new facet. The devotee's practice is to stay porous to that inexhaustibility rather than fixing a final definition.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's 'mat-param' reading keeps a personal anchor in the impersonal: even nirvishesha-Brahman is 'Vishnu's own rupa.' In life, this means: intellectual inquiry into the absolute need not end in abstraction — it can return to a personal devotional center. When philosophical study exhausts the conceptual categories, let it empty into bhakti rather than into nihilism.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's synthesis offers a practical technology for moments of genuine unknowing: when you cannot name what you are experiencing — grief that is not quite grief, joy that exceeds its occasion — resist the urgency to categorize. The experience is pointing beyond both the affirmative and negative frames. This is not pathology; it is the self touching its own ground. The appropriate response is not analysis but attentive stillness.
